text:
X
r
Consensus protocols in distributed ledger systems estab-
a
lish agreements on the messages (or transactions, used in-
terchangeably in this paper) to process in some specific order.
Decentralized applications, particularly those utilizing smart
contracts, often result in different states if messages are pro-
cessed in different orders. Since nodes in distributed ledgers
have significant freedom to censor the messages and decide
on an order, they can manipulate the states of decentralized
applications by altering the order of message processing. In
practice, nodes frequently manipulate message ordering to
maximize rewards. For example, in major blockchains such as
Bitcoin [56] and Ethereum [77], block proposers typically fa-
vor transactions with higher fees. Consequently, transactions
with lower fees experience longer delays, leading to fairness
concerns [70].
1

ir Message Ordering
Evangelos Pournaras
School of Computer Science, University of Leeds
More importantly, in decentralized finance (DeFi) appli-
cations that rely on smart contracts, nodes have economic
incentives to manipulate the order of message processing to
generate additional profits known as Maximal Extractable
Value (MEV), often resulting in substantial financial losses
for users [62]. For example, a node can create a transaction
to buy an asset and prioritize it if the node observes a trans-
action in the message pool that can raise the asset price once
processed. As a result of such profiting strategies through
transaction ordering manipulation, around 200 million USD
are extracted from DeFi users every year [62].
Although there are multiple definitions and approaches
that label message order fairness in existing literature, a com-
monality among these definitions is that they are designed to
prevent MEV, where a single node in the system can decide
message ordering out of its own interest.
The core challenge in deriving a fair ordering of messages
in distributed ledger systems is the state asynchronization
across nodes. As shown in Figure 1, in decentralized networks,
each node can independently receive messages from external
users, resulting in differing local views of the message pool.
Even if all nodes eventually receive the same set of messages
by constantly exchanging their message pool, they are likely
to receive them at varying times and in differing orders due
to network delay and asynchrony [64], making it difficult for
the nodes to reach an agreement of a fair message process-
ing order. An easy way to prevent asynchronous message
pools is to have a centralized entity receiving messages [26],
but it violates the nature of decentralization. Moreover, most
distributed ledger systems require Byzantine fault tolerance
(BFT), which adds additional considerations to the robustness
of the fair ordering rule in designing a fair ordering protocol.
Awareness of the message ordering problem has arisen in
the past five years and has shown a growing interest as the
number of related works grown. While there are proposals
for fair ordering consensus under network asynchrony and
Byzantine-fault nodes, a comprehensive understanding of the
challenges, and design choices of these systems is yet to be
developed. Therefore, we present the first systematization of

metadata.section_title: Preliminaries: Status of Real-world Distributed Ledger Message Ordering

text:
2.1  ProblemModeling
In a distributed ledger, messages are constantly sent from
external users to one or more nodes in the system, or gener-
ated by nodes within the system. Each successful consensus
process commits a new, ordered set of messages to process
at the application layer, as shown in Figure 2. For simplicity,
we assume all nodes have equal likelihoods of receiving mes-
sages and each node has an equal opportunity to participate in
message ordering. That is, messages are neither advantaged
nor disadvantaged based on the nodes that receive them.
2

ion

ordering
Figure O2rd:erMing reulessage flow in distributed ledgers
execution
Nodes are assumed to be rational, meaning they act in their
Ordering results
own interests piefer tvehrifeicaytiond  etect profitable opportunities from ad-
justing message ordering. For instance, if a node is selected as
the leader in a consensus round, it might manipulate message
order for financial gain.
In fair message ordering consensus protocol design, to align
with the common security setting of most existing permission-
less distributed ledgers, the network must tolerate up to f
Byzantine fault nodes out of n total nodes, where n ≥ 3 f +1.
Byzantine nodes may deviate arbitrarily from the protocol
without preventing honest nodes from reaching consensus.
Broadly speaking, this message-ordering challenge applies to
any general model of Byzantine fault-tolerant state machine
replication [30] systems that require consensus on message
ordering.
2.2  Consensus and Message Ordering Imple-
mentation in Existing Systems
Distributed ledgers usually rely on Byzantine fault-tolerant
(BFT) committee-based consensus mechanisms that impose
a total order on messages for sequential execution at the ap-
plication layer. Li et al. [47] listed the transaction sequencing
rules in the consensus protocol implementation of a number
of mainstream distributed ledger systems. It is shown that
their consensus implementations use different parameters to
order messages, including transaction fees, time, and locality
(prioritize local messages). However, these rules are not en-
forced, that is, each node can modify these implementations
to use their own ordering rules. For example, Avalance [67]
is a DAG-based system with a standard implementation that
prioritizes older transactions (FIFO). However, rational nodes
can run an alternative ordering rule if they see MEV profit
opportunities, and the resulting order is still approved by the
peers.
2.3 Maximal Extractable Value
Decentralized finance is a popular category of application that
is built on top of distributed ledgers. Maximal Extractable
Value (MEV) is the profit opportunities nodes can get by lo-
cally observing uncommitted transactions in decentralized fi-
nance applications [37, 62, 90]. Typically, nodes can front-run

been identified as an effective solution to the MEV prob-
lem [12, 80].
3 Message Ordering Rules
This section formalizes different message ordering rules in
the Byzantine fault-tolerant setting and discusses in detail
the requirements of executing these ordering rules and their
limitations.
We define a message ordering rule as a function that sorts
an unordered set of messages into a specific sequence based
m  m
on certain metadata. Let S  and O  be the family of the sets
of m unordered messages and ordered messages (|S| = |O| =
m), respectively. Let D denote the set of metadata used to
order messages (varies depending on the actual ordering rule)
and D ∈ D. Now we define a message ordering rule function:
m  m
r : S ×D −→O
(S,D) (cid:55)−→O
That is, a message ordering rule function orders a set of mes-
sages S, which comes from the aggregation of all local mes-
sage pools of the network nodes, using some metadata D and
outputs the messages in an ordered list (s.t. ∀ tx ∈ O, tx ∈ S).
Below, we discuss in detail the types of ordering rules r and
the corresponding metadata D used to decide the ordering of
the messages in these rules.
3.1  FIFO Ordering Rule
The most-studied ordering rule is FIFO ordering [6,11,16,25,
34, 35, 40–42, 44–46, 63, 69, 70, 74, 76, 78, 82, 83, 87], which
seeks to process messages at a first-come-first-commit man-
ner.
As first mentioned by Kelkar et al. [42], it is impossible
to measure the time when external messages are sent to the
systemwithout making additional assumptions on the network
status of message senders or having independent trustworthy
timestamping services. Thus, FIFO ordering primarily targets
processing messages as they are first received by the system
nodes.
3

Figure 3: An example of Condorcet cycle from ranked voting
However, since messages can be received by different nodes
at different times and orders at the network layer, the major
challenge of FIFO ordering rules is to derive a fair global
message order using these different local views. Moreover, in
the Byzantine fault-tolerant setting, the rules have to consider
that up to f local views are missing or indistinguishably ma-
licious. Two main methods to achieve FIFO ordering under
these conditions are ranked voting and median timestamping.
3.1.1  FIFO via Ranked Voting
The process of collectively agreeing on an order when each
voter node has a different local order preference can be mod-
eled by ranked-choice voting. Therefore, there are some pro-
posals to derive the order of messages by collecting ranked
local message arrival orders from each individual node and
aggregating them [6,15,16,40–42,44,76]. Different from nor-
mal ranked-choice voting aggregation, the algorithms have to
account for that not all messages are received by all nodes, and
therefore the local rankings are incomplete from the global
perspective.
S
Given an unordered message set S, let Oi be the ordered
set of messages based on the arrival order of messages locally
observed by node i. FIFO order via voting is defined as the
following ordering rule:
S  S  S
FIFO(S, [O1,O2, ...,On])
In FIFO via ranked voting, each node’s local order of mes-
sages acts as a “ballot” that ranks the messages, and the fi-
nal ordering is decided by majority preference among nodes.
However, this method may not always yield a total FIFO or-
der due to the cases of draw Condorcet cycles [32], where
cyclic preferences among nodes are found [42]. Figure 3 is a
demonstration of how a Condorcet cycle is formed in ranked
voting. In this example, the majority of the local views sug-
gest message 1 comes before message 2, message 2 comes
before message 3, but also believe message 3 comes before
message 1.

Even worse, such cycles can be infinitely extended.
This raises an attack opportunity of deliberately creating or
extending Condorcet cycles in the case where malicious ex-
ternal message senders submit messages to different nodes at
carefully designed times [74].
To tolerate Condorcet cycles, different relaxed notions of
order fairness are proposed. Block order fairness [42] (later
referred to as batch order fairness) and similarly differential
order fairness [16] are proposed that allow multiple messages

to be deemed as received at the same time. In this case, any
ordering among these messages is deemed acceptable.
As Condorcet cycles can be arbitrarily long, making sure
messages in Condorcet cycles commit at the same time also
impacts the liveness of the protocol [42]. A solution to this
problem is to assume an external synchrony time bound [42],
so that when the time of receiving two messages is over the
time bound, they must not be in a Condorcet cycle. The draw-
back of this method is the high latency to commit a mes-
sage and the requirement of the additional external synchrony
assumption. To improve on that, one way is to use ranked
pairs [73] to break Condorcet cycles without violating FIFO
order fairness [63, 74]. Themis [41] also introduces another
method called batch-unspooling to commit messages without
requiring to observe full cycles.
To summarize, this line of research attempts to handle Con-
dorcet cycles in a way that minimizes their effect on latency.
However, taking a step back, improvements can also be made
by actively preventing the formation of Condorcet cycles.
Therefore, a potential research direction that can advance
FIFO via voting proposals is to reduce the probability of
forming Condorcet cycles, for example, by requiring more
frequent message propagation [74].
3.1.2  FIFO via Timestamping
Another type of proposal for FIFO ordering is to assign a
timestamp to each message and order based on the assigned
timestamps [11, 25, 34, 35, 45, 46, 78, 82, 83, 87, 88].
Based on the existing proposals, there are two ways to
timestamp a message. The first way is to make Byzantine
agreements using the median value of the timestamps that the
nodes locally receive for a message [11, 25, 34, 35, 45, 46, 87].
The median value is used because it eliminates the effect of
the malicious timestamp proposals from the Byzantine nodes.
The median timestamp is always within the range of time the
message was received by the honest nodes.
S
Given a set of messages S, let Ti  denote the list of times-
tamps at which every message of S is locally observed by
node i. FIFO ordering via median timestamping is defined as
the following ordering rule:
S  S  S
FIFO(S, [T1 ,T2 , ...,Tn ])
Table 1 provides an example scenario of FIFO via median
timestamping. Based on the decided timestamp, message 1
is ordered before message 2. However, a problem with using
timestamps from a single node (the median) is that the node
can manipulate the relative ordering of transactions. If node 2
is malicious, it can change the order of the messages simply
by sharing the flipped local timestamps for receiving the two
messages. Although it is believed that such manipulation is
difficult in practice for an adversary [45, 87] as it requires an
adversary to submit a timestamp that is exactly the median, the
possibility of such manipulation cannot be fully eliminated.
4

Table 1: An example of taking median timestamp to order
messages
Message  Node 1  Node 2  Node 3  Output
Msg 1  3:00  3:02  3:04  3:02
Msg 2  3:01  3:03  3:05  3:03
Using some median timestamp tm implies that it comes
from one single node (or two nodes) in the network. When-
ever tm is used to compare with timestamps from any other
nodes in the network, there must be an assumption that these
two local clocks are synchronized, otherwise the comparison
does not provide any conclusions. Since all existing median
timestamping proposals involve cross-node timestamp com-
parisons, they all require synchronized clocks. This does not
mean median timestamping methods are infeasible, as BFT
clock synchronization protocol exists and it can be applied to
ensure synchronized clocks [50, 60].
Byzantine agreement of the median value given a set of
values has been extensively studied [72], and it is also used in
this line of research to find out the best median value among
the honest nodes [25]. However, we argue that the problem
here is somewhat different from finding a median value. In-
stead of finding the median value among honest proposals,
the aim of this problem is to find the minimum honest value,
as it is the closest timestamp to when the message is actually
sent.

This brings up a research gap that has not yet been stud-
ied, that is, how accurate the timestamps derived from these
protocols are from the actual time when the message is sent
by the sender, and how to minimize the deviation between the
actual and derived timestamps.
Apart from median timestamping, another method to de-
rive timestamps for messages is to infer the message arrival
timestamp according to the known network delay between
the peers [82, 83]. This requires an additional assumption that
the network delay remains relatively stable.
Given a set of messages S, let L denote a mapping of net-
work latency from all combinations of source and destination
nodes. Isrc is the list of source nodes that broadcast S. FIFO
ordering via inference timestamping is defined as:
FIFO(S,L, Isrc)
In this category of approach, both proposals [82,83] assume
a message is broadcasted from one node to all other nodes.
In the case of unstable network delay and the network is not
synchronized, nodes can not reach an agreement because the
resulting timestamping is not within the acceptable range of
their expectation, therefore liveness is lost. Given such strict
conditions, the scalability and resilience of this approach is
yet to be verified..

metadata.section_title: Fair Consensus Factory: Adding Fairness into Consensus Protocols

text:
into Consensus Protocols
To apply the fair ordering rules in a distributed ledger, the
nodes need to reach an agreement on the order results, namely,
to embed fair ordering rules into the consensus protocols. This
section presents the fair consensus factory, a framework to add
order fairness to any byzantine fault-tolerant committee-based
consensus protocol, which is commonly used in distributed
ledgers.
4.1  Selecting Fair Ordering Rules
Before bringing a consensus into the factory, a decision to
make is to select which ordering rules to apply. Figure 4
shows the fair ordering options a consensus protocol has. A
consensus can either use random ordering, FIFO via ranked
voting, or FIFO via timestamping, with an optional add-on
of requiring the nodes to apply the ordering rule without
knowing their content. Details of these options are covered in
the previous section.
4.2  Process of Adding Fairness
Before executing fair ordering rules, it requires a stage to
synchronize the local states among the nodes including the
local message pools and the required metadata. Subsequently,
all nodes can execute the ordering rule to derive the ordered
message set of this round that they should commit. This is not
the only possible workflow for fair ordering consensus. There
are also proposals [2, 38, 47, 71] to use trusted execution envi-
ronment (TEE) message pools to enforce transaction selection

rules, so that only the leader of that consensus round needs
to execute the rule and others can trust the result is correct.
However, this creates centralization risks to trust the hardware
manufacturer [84], which is not desired in the decentralized
setting. Another potential way that only requires one node to
execute the orderingHrouw ltoe oridser tmoesscargees ate zero-knowledge proof

quiring the leader ttoimgesetanmepriangte proofs is expected to contribute
significantly to higher latency.
Therefore, the fair consensus factory sticks to the approach
of all-to-all synchOrpotnioinzali andgd-,oan:n bdlinda lolrdneroindges execute rules locally.
It takes three steps to modify a normal consensus protocol to
achieve so:
Set the propose stage redundant  Without an enforced
ordering rule, normal consensus protocols allow nodes to
freely propose an ordered message set at a propose stage.
This stage is not needed in a fair ordering consensus because
the message set to commit should be derived deterministically
from the ordering rules. Therefore, the first step of the process
is to remove the propose stage.
Install the Synchronization Stage  Normal consensus can
be executed with non-synchronized message pools for each
node. However, if message pools are not synchronized in fair
ordering consensus different local views of the message pool
greatly increase the opportunity of getting unfair results (as
shown in almost all existing proposals) and may be prone to
attacks [74]. Therefore, a synchronization stage is required
for nodes to update each others’ local message pools before
ordering to share the same view of messages. If the con-
sensus is to be processed into a FIFO ordering consensus,
this synchronization stage also involves exchanging the local
metadata (detailed in the previous section) required to execute
the ordering rule.
Note that in FIFO ordering, nodes are expected to not only
synchronize in this synchronization stage of a consensus pro-
cess, but also to constantly exchange local messages at the
network layer before starting a consensus process. This is to
populate the metadata (e.g. timestamps of locally receiving
the messages for FIFO via timestamping rules) to be used in
the consensus process.
Install the Rule Execution Stage  The purpose of the above
state synchronization stage is to allow all nodes to execute the
fair ordering rule with the same input message set and meta-
data.

commit to in this consensus round. Unlike the synchronization
stage which requires all-to-all communication to exchange
local states, this stage requires only local computations for
each node. After this stage, normal consensus stages can be
applied to reach an agreement, commonly referred to as the
pre-commit and commit phase.
4.3  Different  Processing  Approaches  for
Leader-based and Leaderless Consensus
Leader-based [8, 41, 87] (to name a few, the majority of con-
sensus used in distributed ledgers are leader-based) and lead-
erless [7, 10, 27, 33, 42, 67] are two categories of committee-
based consensus protocols. This categorization makes a differ-
ence in fairness consensus factory because it requires different
approaches to add order fairness.
In leader-based consensus, only the leader proposes an
ordered set of messages and communicates to the peers, there-
fore, the factory simply removes the propose phase and re-
places it with local rule executions. However, in a leaderless
consensus, all nodes can propose in a round. These proposals
are aggregated to derive a final ordered message set with a
deterministic algorithm to commit. Therefore, when handling
leaderless consensus, the factory removes both the propose
phase and the aggregation phase, turning them into the same
synchronize-and-execute flow as in the leader-based consen-
sus.
In both leader-based and leaderless consensus, state syn-
chronization can be integrated into the communication during.

Themis [41] is a consensus protocol with a FIFO-via-voting
algorithm.  It is recognized as a potential solution to the
blockchain order-fairness problem [17, 37, 61, 80]. In par-
ticular, Chen et al. [17] present execution time optimization
for the executionHoowr dtoe orridnegr malegssoargietsh m used in Themis. We pick
up this state-of-the-art consensus and apply the fair consen-
sus factory framework to demonRstrate the framework’s appli-

cability to a fair ordering protocol and potentially provides
performance optimizations at the consensus level.

6.2  Public Verifiability of Order Fairness
In existing fair ordering proposals, external parties are unable
to verify whether the ordering results are fair. Without such
verifiable fairness, there is a risk for the majority of nodes in
the system to collude and corrupt only the order fairness in
the consensus, which is non-detectable without verifiability.
That is, from the view of the message senders, it is indis-
tinguishable whether a consensus provides order fairness or
not.
The only related work on verifiability is presented by Li
et al. [47], where they provide a protocol to enable message
senders to verify whether their messages are correctly pro-
cessed and ordered by the nodes using an extra round of
communication for nodes to send receipts to message senders

This paper explores fair message ordering in distributed
ledgers, addressing a critical need for fairness in distributed
ledgers where manipulation of transaction order, such as Max-
imal Extractable Value (MEV), becomes a prominent problem.
By examining FIFO, random, and blind ordering, we outline
the challenges and trade-offs of implementing fair ordering in
Byzantine fault-tolerant settings. Our proposed Fair Consen-
sus Factory framework enables flexible integration of fairness
into consensus protocols and was demonstrated through opti-
mization of the FIFO ordering consensus protocol, Themis, to
reduce latency. This approach shows the potential of modular
fairness frameworks to improve both performance and equity
in distributed systems. Having a better understanding of fair
message ordering accelerates the adoption of blockchain tech-
nologies. Our finding provides good guidance for researchers
in this area by providing a systematic understanding of exist-
ing works and identifying future research directions.
